walkthrough.title: Gather the Tiles
walkthrough.text: Let's get Riichi Mahjong set up. First, find all one hundred thirty-six tiles. There are three suits of numbered tiles — Characters, Circles, and Bamboo — each numbered one through nine with four copies of every tile. Then there are honor tiles: four Wind tiles and three Dragon tiles, also with four copies each. Make sure you have all of them before we start.

walkthrough.title: Identify the Suits
walkthrough.text: Let's quickly identify the three suits so nobody gets confused. Characters have Chinese numerals with a character symbol on the bottom. Circles have round dots arranged in patterns, kind of like a domino. Bamboo tiles show sticks or rods, except the one of Bamboo which usually shows a bird. Take a moment to sort a few and make sure everyone can tell the three suits apart.

walkthrough.title: Identify the Honor Tiles
walkthrough.text: Now let's look at the honor tiles. There are four Wind tiles: East, South, West, and North. Each has a Chinese character on it, and there are four copies of each wind. Then there are three Dragon tiles: White, Green, and Red. White is usually blank or has a decorative border. Green has the character for prosperity. Red has the character for center. That makes twenty-eight honor tiles total.

walkthrough.title: Get the Scoring Sticks
walkthrough.text: Find the scoring sticks, called tenbo. These are thin sticks with colored dots that represent points. There are four denominations: ten thousand point sticks, five thousand point sticks, one thousand point sticks, and one hundred point sticks. Each denomination has a different dot pattern so you can tell them apart at a glance. Set them all in a central area where everyone can reach them.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Points
walkthrough.text: Each player starts with twenty-five thousand points in scoring sticks. The standard breakdown is one ten thousand stick, two five thousand sticks, four one thousand sticks, and ten one hundred sticks. Count them out and give each player their set. Keep your sticks visible in front of you throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Find the Dice and Markers
walkthrough.text: You will need two standard six-sided dice and a round wind marker. The wind marker shows which round you are in — East or South. Some sets also include a small seat wind indicator for the dealer. Set the dice and marker in the center of the table where the dealer can reach them.

walkthrough.title: Choose Seats
walkthrough.text: In traditional Riichi Mahjong, seating is determined randomly. The simplest way is to shuffle one of each wind tile face down, then have each player draw one. East sits anywhere they like, then South sits to East's right, West sits across from East, and North sits to East's left. Remember, play goes counterclockwise in Mahjong, which is opposite to most Western games.

walkthrough.title: Determine the Dealer
walkthrough.text: The player who drew the East wind tile becomes the first dealer. The dealer is always the East seat for the current hand. Being the dealer is important because the dealer scores one and a half times the normal amount when they win. Set the round wind marker to East to show that you are starting the East round.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle All Tiles Face Down
walkthrough.text: Now take all one hundred thirty-six tiles and flip them face down on the table. Mix them thoroughly by pushing them around with both hands. This is called washing the tiles. Make sure everything is well randomized. Everyone should help with the mixing to speed things up.

walkthrough.title: Build the Walls
walkthrough.text: Each player takes thirty-four face-down tiles and builds them into a wall in front of themselves. The wall should be seventeen tiles long and two tiles high, with all tiles still face down. When all four players have built their walls, push them together to form a large square in the center of the table. This square represents the Great Wall from which tiles will be drawn.

walkthrough.title: Break the Wall
walkthrough.text: The dealer rolls the two dice. Add the dice together and count that many stacks from the right end of the dealer's own wall. Separate the wall at that point — the tiles to the right of the break become the start of the dead wall, and dealing begins from the left side of the break. This determines where the deal starts and where the dead wall sits.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Tiles
walkthrough.text: Starting from the break point, deal tiles counterclockwise beginning with the dealer. Take four tiles at a time — two stacks — for each player, going around the table three times. That gives everyone twelve tiles. Then deal one more tile to each player, but give the dealer one extra — the dealer ends up with fourteen tiles, everyone else gets thirteen.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Dead Wall
walkthrough.text: Count seven stacks — fourteen tiles — from the break point going to the right. These fourteen tiles form the dead wall. The dead wall is never drawn from during normal play — it is only used for replacement draws after declaring a kan. Slightly separate the dead wall from the rest of the wall so everyone can see where it begins.

walkthrough.title: Flip the Dora Indicator
walkthrough.text: On the dead wall, count three tiles from the far end — the end away from the live wall — and flip the top tile face up. This tile is the dora indicator. It does not directly become dora — instead, the next tile in sequence is the dora. For example, if the indicator is a five of Circles, then the six of Circles is the dora. Each dora in your winning hand adds bonus scoring value.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Dora Sequence
walkthrough.text: The dora sequence wraps around within each tile group. For suited tiles, the sequence goes one through nine and then wraps back to one. So if the indicator is a nine of Bamboo, the one of Bamboo is the dora. For winds, the order is East, South, West, North, and it wraps back to East. For dragons, the order is White, Green, Red, wrapping back to White.

walkthrough.title: Sort Your Hand
walkthrough.text: Pick up your tiles and arrange them so only you can see them. Sort them by suit — all Characters together, then Circles, then Bamboo, then honors at one end. Within each suit, arrange them in numerical order. This makes it much easier to spot potential melds and plan your strategy. The dealer should have fourteen tiles, and everyone else should have thirteen.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Your Discard Area
walkthrough.text: Each player needs a clear space in front of them, between their hand and the wall, for discards. Throughout the hand, you will place discarded tiles face up in neat rows of six in this area. Your discard pool is public information and extremely important — it tells other players what you do not need and affects the furiten rule. Keep it organized.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: Setup is complete. The dealer has fourteen tiles and will start the hand by discarding one tile. The other three players each have thirteen tiles and will draw from the wall on their turn. The round wind is East, the dora indicator is showing, and everyone has twenty-five thousand points. Let's learn how to play.

walkthrough.title: Goal of the Game
walkthrough.text: The goal of Riichi Mahjong is to build a complete winning hand of fourteen tiles before anyone else. A standard winning hand consists of four melds plus one pair. A meld is either a sequence of three consecutive tiles in the same suit or a triplet of three identical tiles. You also need at least one scoring pattern, called a yaku, to declare a win. Let's break down how each turn works.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure: Draw and Discard
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you draw one tile from the live wall, add it to your hand, and then discard one tile face up into your discard area. That is the basic rhythm — draw one, discard one. You always want to end your turn with thirteen tiles in your hand, unless you are the dealer on the very first turn, who starts with fourteen and just discards.

walkthrough.title: Play Direction
walkthrough.text: Play moves counterclockwise around the table. After the dealer discards, the player to the dealer's right draws and discards, then the next player to the right, and so on. This is the opposite of most Western card games, so it can feel backwards at first. Just remember: the player on your right goes after you, not the player on your left.

walkthrough.title: Sequences and Triplets
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the two types of melds you can build. A sequence is three consecutive tiles in the same suit, like two, three, and four of Circles. You can never use honor tiles in a sequence. A triplet is three identical tiles, like three copies of the East wind or three copies of the seven of Bamboo. Any tile can form a triplet. You need four melds total to win.

walkthrough.title: The Pair
walkthrough.text: In addition to your four melds, you need exactly one pair — two identical tiles. Any tile can serve as the pair. The pair is sometimes called the head of the hand. So your winning hand is four melds of three tiles each, which is twelve tiles, plus one pair of two tiles, for fourteen tiles total.

walkthrough.title: Calling Chi for a Sequence
walkthrough.text: When the player to your left discards a tile that would complete a sequence in your hand, you can call chi to claim it. Say chi, reveal the two tiles from your hand that form the sequence with the discarded tile, and place all three face up in front of you. Then discard a tile from your hand. Calling chi opens your hand, which limits which scoring patterns you can use. Only the player whose turn is next may call chi.

walkthrough.title: Calling Pon for a Triplet
walkthrough.text: When any player discards a tile and you have two copies of it in your hand, you can call pon to claim it and form a triplet. Say pon, reveal your two matching tiles, and place all three face up in front of you. Then discard a tile. Unlike chi, any player can call pon regardless of seating position. Pon has higher priority than chi, so if two players want the same discard, pon wins.

walkthrough.title: Open vs Closed Hands
walkthrough.text: This is a crucial concept. A closed hand is one where you have not called chi or pon — all your tiles were drawn from the wall. An open hand has at least one called meld sitting face up in front of you. Many of the best scoring patterns require a closed hand. Opening your hand is not always bad, but it limits your options. Think carefully before calling.

walkthrough.title: What is a Yaku?
walkthrough.text: A yaku is a specific scoring pattern that your hand must contain in order to win. You need at least one yaku to declare a winning hand. There are dozens of different yaku, each worth a certain number of han, which are scoring multipliers. Some yaku are simple, like having all tiles in the two through eight range. Others are more complex. You cannot win without at least one yaku, even if your hand has four melds and a pair.

walkthrough.title: Easy Yaku: Riichi
walkthrough.text: Riichi is the signature yaku of this game and one of the easiest to achieve. When your hand is one tile away from winning — a state called tenpai — and your hand is fully closed, you can declare riichi. Place a one thousand point stick on the table as a bet. After declaring riichi, you cannot change your hand. You must discard every tile you draw unless it completes your win. Riichi is worth one han.

walkthrough.title: Easy Yaku: Tanyao
walkthrough.text: Tanyao, also called all simples, is the most beginner-friendly yaku. Your entire hand must contain only tiles numbered two through eight — no ones, no nines, no wind tiles, and no dragon tiles. If every tile in your hand, including the pair, is a simple tile, that is tanyao. It is worth one han and works in both open and closed hands in most rule sets.

walkthrough.title: Easy Yaku: Yakuhai
walkthrough.text: Yakuhai means having a triplet of certain honor tiles. Specifically, a triplet of any dragon tile — White, Green, or Red — is worth one han each. A triplet of the round wind is worth one han, and a triplet of your own seat wind is also worth one han. If the round wind matches your seat wind, one triplet counts for two han. This yaku works with open or closed hands.

walkthrough.title: Easy Yaku: Pinfu
walkthrough.text: Pinfu is a closed-hand-only yaku worth one han. Your hand must contain only sequences, no triplets. The pair cannot be a dragon, round wind, or seat wind. And you must be waiting on two different tiles to complete your last sequence, called an open wait. For example, if you have four and five of Bamboo and need either three or six to complete the sequence, that qualifies.

walkthrough.title: Declaring Tsumo
walkthrough.text: When you draw a tile from the wall that completes your winning hand, you declare tsumo. Reveal your entire hand and announce the win. With a tsumo win, all three other players pay you. The non-dealer players each pay the base amount, and the dealer pays double. Tsumo with a closed hand is itself a yaku worth one han, called menzen tsumo.

walkthrough.title: Declaring Ron
walkthrough.text: When another player discards a tile that would complete your winning hand, you can declare ron. Say ron immediately, before the next player draws. Reveal your hand to confirm the win. With a ron win, only the player who discarded the winning tile pays the full amount. Ron is not a yaku by itself — you still need at least one yaku from your hand's composition.

walkthrough.title: The Furiten Rule
walkthrough.text: Furiten is one of the most important rules in Riichi Mahjong. If any tile that would complete your hand is already in your own discard pile, you are in furiten. While in furiten, you cannot win by ron — you can only win by tsumo. This prevents players from discarding a tile and then later winning off someone else's discard of the same tile. Always check your discards before declaring ron.

walkthrough.title: Temporary Furiten
walkthrough.text: There is also temporary furiten. If another player discards a tile that would complete your hand and you choose not to call ron, you enter temporary furiten. You cannot call ron on any player's discard until your next turn comes around and you draw a tile. This rule prevents you from waiting to cherry-pick a specific player to pay you. If you pass on a winning discard, you must wait.

walkthrough.title: Declaring Kan
walkthrough.text: If you have four identical tiles, you can declare a kan to form a quad. There are three types. A closed kan uses four tiles from your hand — place them face down with the two middle tiles flipped up to show it is a set. An open kan starts from calling a discard when you hold three matching tiles. An added kan adds a drawn fourth tile to an existing open triplet. After any kan, draw a replacement tile from the dead wall and flip a new dora indicator.

walkthrough.title: Dora Bonus Tiles
walkthrough.text: Remember the dora indicator we flipped during setup. The tile that comes next in sequence after the indicator is the dora. Each dora tile in your winning hand adds one han to your score. If you declare kan, an additional dora indicator is flipped, potentially adding more dora to the game. Dora add han but are not yaku by themselves — you still need at least one real yaku to win.

walkthrough.title: Ura Dora
walkthrough.text: If you win after declaring riichi, you get to check the ura dora. Flip the tile directly underneath each dora indicator on the dead wall. These tiles indicate additional dora the same way — the next tile in sequence is the ura dora. This can dramatically boost your score and is one of the reasons riichi is such a popular declaration.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Basics: Han and Fu
walkthrough.text: Scoring in Riichi Mahjong uses two values: han and fu. Han are your scoring multipliers, which come from yaku and dora. Fu are base points calculated from the specific composition of your hand — things like the type of wait, whether melds are open or closed, and what your pair is. The formula multiplies fu by powers of two based on han, but do not worry about memorizing the math. Use a scoring table or app.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Thresholds
walkthrough.text: As han increase, scoring jumps to fixed thresholds. Mangan is reached at three han with seventy or more fu, or four han with thirty or more fu. Above mangan, the thresholds are haneman at six to seven han, baiman at eight to ten han, sanbaiman at eleven to twelve han, and yakuman at thirteen or more han. In practice, most beginner hands will be one to three han. A mangan hand feels like a big win.

walkthrough.title: Payment Between Players
walkthrough.text: When someone wins by tsumo, all three opponents pay. The non-dealer players each pay a base amount, and the dealer pays double that base. When someone wins by ron, only the discarder pays the full amount. If the dealer wins, they receive more points overall. Payments are made by exchanging scoring sticks. Use a scoring table to look up exact amounts by han and fu.

walkthrough.title: Exhaustive Draw
walkthrough.text: If the wall runs out of tiles and nobody has won, the hand ends in an exhaustive draw. The last fourteen tiles are always the dead wall and are never drawn. When a draw happens, each player reveals whether they are tenpai or not. Tenpai players collect a share of three thousand points from non-tenpai players. If everyone or nobody is tenpai, no points change hands.

walkthrough.title: Dealer Rotation
walkthrough.text: After each hand, the dealer may rotate. If the dealer won the hand or was tenpai during a draw, the dealer stays and a bonus counter is added — this is called renchan. The bonus counter means additional points are at stake in the next hand. If the dealer did not win and was not tenpai at a draw, the dealership passes to the next player counterclockwise.

walkthrough.title: Game Structure: Rounds and Hands
walkthrough.text: A full game consists of at least two rounds: the East round and the South round. Each round has four hands, one for each player's turn as dealer. That means a minimum of eight hands per game. If the dealer repeats, additional hands are played within that round. The round wind changes from East to South after all four players have been dealer once.

walkthrough.title: Game End Conditions
walkthrough.text: The game normally ends after the South round is complete. However, if any player's points drop below zero, the game ends immediately. Some rule sets also end the game if a player exceeds thirty thousand points after a dealer's winning hand in the South round. The player with the most points at the end wins.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring and Placement
walkthrough.text: At the end of the game, final standings are determined by point totals. First place is the player with the most points. In many tournament rules, an additional placement bonus is applied — first place gets a bonus, last place gets a penalty. For casual play, simply comparing point totals is enough. The margin of victory matters, so every hand counts even if you are in the lead.

Must have at least 1 yaku (scoring pattern) to win

Player to your right goes after you

No yaku = no win, even with 4 melds + pair

summary.title: Easy Yaku: Riichi

Worth 1 han; works open or closed

If seat wind = round wind, that triplet = 2 han
Works open or closed

bullets: Closed hand only; all sequences, no triplets
Pair cannot be dragons, round wind, or seat wind
Must have an open (two-sided) wait; worth 1 han

summary.title: Declaring Tsumo
bullets: Draw from wall completes your hand → declare tsumo
All 3 opponents pay (dealer pays double)

bullets: Opponent's discard completes your hand → declare ron immediately
Only the discarder pays full amount
Ron is not a yaku — you still need 1+ yaku from hand composition

bullets: If any winning tile is in your own discard pile → furiten
Furiten = cannot win by ron; tsumo only
Always check your discards before declaring ron

bullets: Pass on a ron opportunity → cannot ron until your next draw
Prevents cherry-picking which opponent pays

Not a yaku by itself — still need 1+ real yaku

bullets: Win with riichi → flip tile under each dora indicator

summary.title: Scoring Basics: Han and Fu
bullets: Han = multipliers from yaku + dora
Fu = base points from hand composition, waits, melds
Score = fu × 2^(2+han); use a scoring table

summary.title: Scoring Thresholds
bullets: Mangan: 3 han/70fu or 4 han/30fu+
Haneman: 6-7 han; Baiman: 8-10 han

summary.title: Payment Between Players
bullets: Tsumo win: all 3 opponents pay (dealer pays 2×)

bullets: Wall empty (14 dead wall tiles remain) → draw
Tenpai players receive share of 3,000 from non-tenpai
All tenpai or all non-tenpai → no point change

Each round = 4 hands (1 per player as dealer)

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Hand
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first hand of Riichi Mahjong! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted hand with four players — East, South, West, and North. We will follow the action from East's perspective as the dealer, but we will peek at other players' decisions to see common situations. Don't worry about memorizing every yaku yet. Just follow the flow of draw, think, discard.

walkthrough.title: East's Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: East, you are the dealer and you have fourteen tiles. Let's say your hand has a pair of Red Dragons, a partial sequence in Circles with a three and four, several scattered Bamboo tiles, some Characters, and a couple of wind tiles. Your first job is to sort everything by suit and look for tiles that are already close to forming melds. The pair of Red Dragons is great — that is already the start of a yakuhai triplet.

walkthrough.title: East's First Discard
walkthrough.text: As the dealer, you start with fourteen tiles and need to discard one to begin the hand. Look for tiles that are isolated — not close to forming any meld. A lone North wind that is not the round wind or your seat wind is a safe first discard. You place it face up in your discard area. Now you have thirteen tiles, and play moves to South.

walkthrough.title: South Draws and Discards
walkthrough.text: South draws a tile from the wall, checks their hand, and discards an isolated one of Characters. Notice how South also started by throwing away an isolated terminal tile. Terminals — ones and nines — are harder to use in sequences because they can only connect in one direction. Early discards of terminals and isolated honors are very common.

walkthrough.title: West's Mistake: Calling Chi Too Early
walkthrough.text: West sees South's discarded one of Characters and has a two and three of Characters in hand. West excitedly calls chi to form a one-two-three sequence. The meld goes face up in front of West. But here is the problem: West has now opened their hand on the very first tile. This means West cannot declare riichi, cannot score pinfu, and has lost access to several valuable closed-hand-only yaku.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Keep Your Hand Closed
walkthrough.text: That chi was a mistake. In the early hand, you almost always want to keep your hand closed. Calling chi or pon to form one meld is rarely worth the yaku you lose access to. The only good reasons to open early are if you already have a guaranteed yaku like a dragon triplet, or if you are very close to winning and the discard completes a critical meld. Be patient and draw from the wall instead.

walkthrough.title: North Draws, East Draws Again
walkthrough.text: North draws and discards a West wind. Then it is East's turn again. You draw a tile from the wall and it is a Red Dragon — now you have three Red Dragons! That is a yakuhai triplet, worth one han. This is excellent because it guarantees you have a yaku no matter what else your hand looks like. Keep all three hidden in your hand for now.

walkthrough.title: Building Your Hand
walkthrough.text: Over the next few turns, focus on building toward four melds plus a pair. You already have a triplet of Red Dragons forming one meld. Your three and four of Circles need a two or five to become a sequence. Look at each draw and ask: does this tile bring me closer to completing a meld? If not, is it at least a useful connector? Discard the least useful tile each turn.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Discards
walkthrough.text: As tiles accumulate in the discard pools, start paying attention. If you see three copies of the five of Bamboo already discarded by various players, the fourth one is the only one left — waiting for it is almost hopeless. If no Circles have been discarded, Circles are probably being hoarded by your opponents. Reading discards helps you know which waits are alive and which are dead.

walkthrough.title: South Declares Riichi
walkthrough.text: A few rounds in, South places a one thousand point stick sideways on the table and announces riichi. This means South is one tile from winning with a closed hand. South turns their next discard sideways in their discard row to mark when riichi was declared. The mood at the table changes — now everyone needs to think about defense.

walkthrough.title: West's Mistake: Ignoring the Riichi
walkthrough.text: West draws a tile and, ignoring South's riichi declaration, discards a middle tile that they don't need for their own hand. This is dangerous. A player in riichi has a live winning hand, and discarding a tile they need means you pay the full ron penalty. West should have checked whether the tile was safe before discarding it.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Defend Against Riichi
walkthrough.text: When someone declares riichi, you must consider defense. The safest discards are tiles that the riichi player has already discarded — these are guaranteed safe because of the furiten rule. Tiles discarded right after the riichi declaration by other players who were not called on are also generally safe. Avoid discarding tiles in suits the riichi player has not discarded, especially middle tiles like four, five, and six.

walkthrough.title: East Decides: Push or Fold?
walkthrough.text: It is your turn and South is in riichi. You are also pretty close to winning — you need just two more tiles to complete your hand. Should you push forward and keep discarding toward your own win, or should you fold and discard only safe tiles? This is the fundamental push-versus-fold decision in Riichi Mahjong. With a Red Dragon triplet giving you guaranteed yaku and a decent hand, it might be worth pushing.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Push vs Fold
walkthrough.text: The push-versus-fold decision depends on three things. First, how close are you to winning? One tile away is worth pushing, three tiles away is not. Second, how valuable is your hand? If you have three or more han, the reward justifies more risk. Third, how dangerous are your discards? If you have safe tiles to discard while still progressing, you can push without much risk. When in doubt as a beginner, folding is usually correct.

walkthrough.title: East Reaches Tenpai
walkthrough.text: You draw a five of Circles, which completes your third sequence. Now your hand is tenpai — one tile from winning! You have the Red Dragon triplet, three completed sequences, and you need one more tile to complete your pair or final meld. Since your hand is fully closed, you have the option to declare riichi yourself.

walkthrough.title: East Declares Riichi
walkthrough.text: Let's declare riichi. Place a one thousand point stick on the table and discard a tile sideways. Now you are locked in — you cannot change your hand. But you gain the riichi yaku worth one han, plus the Red Dragon yakuhai for another han, giving you at least two han. If you win, you also get to check ura dora for potential bonus han. The risk is that you just committed one thousand points and cannot play defensively anymore.

walkthrough.title: North's Mistake: Discarding into Riichi
walkthrough.text: North draws and discards a tile without checking whether it is safe against East's riichi or South's riichi. It happens to be a tile East is waiting on. East declares ron! North made the classic beginner mistake of not reading the table. With two players in riichi, North should have been extremely cautious and discarded only tiles that both riichi players had already thrown.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Check Before You Discard
walkthrough.text: Every single discard should be a conscious decision. Before placing a tile in your discard area, mentally scan the table. Has this tile already been discarded by the player in riichi? Is it a terminal or honor that appears safe? How many riichi declarations are active? The few seconds you spend checking can save you thousands of points. Careless discards are the number one way beginners lose points.

walkthrough.title: Scoring the Win
walkthrough.text: East won by ron against North. Let's count the score. East has riichi for one han and Red Dragon yakuhai for one han — that is two han minimum. Let's say East also has a dora tile in the hand, making it three han. With the fu from the hand composition, this could be worth around four thousand points from North as a dealer ron. North pays East using scoring sticks, and the hand is over.

walkthrough.title: After the Hand
walkthrough.text: Since East won as dealer, East stays as dealer for the next hand — this is renchan. A bonus counter is added, which means an extra three hundred points will be at stake in the next hand. All tiles are now washed face down again, walls are rebuilt, and a new hand begins. The game continues until all rounds are complete.

walkthrough.title: Common Beginner Trap: No Yaku Hand
walkthrough.text: Here is a mistake that catches every beginner at least once. You have four melds and a pair, you see a winning tile, you excitedly declare ron or tsumo, and then someone checks your hand and discovers you have no yaku. This is called a chombo — a penalty for a false win. Before declaring a win, always confirm that your hand contains at least one yaku. If you opened your hand with chi and pon, double-check, because many easy yaku require a closed hand.

walkthrough.title: Common Beginner Trap: Forgetting Furiten
walkthrough.text: Another painful beginner trap is declaring ron while in furiten. You discarded a seven of Bamboo three turns ago, and now another player discards a seven of Bamboo that would complete your hand. You call ron, but your opponents point out that the seven of Bamboo is in your own discard pile. You are in furiten and cannot win by ron. Always scan your own discards before calling ron.

walkthrough.title: Common Beginner Trap: Opening for No Reason
walkthrough.text: Beginners often call chi or pon on the first tile that could complete any meld, even when there is no strategic reason. Every call opens your hand and reduces your scoring potential. Ask yourself before every call: does this bring me significantly closer to winning, and do I still have a valid yaku after opening? If the answer to either question is no, let the tile pass and draw from the wall instead.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations — you have played through your first hand of Riichi Mahjong! You have seen how to draw and discard, how to build toward melds, how riichi works, how ron and tsumo wins are declared, and how to think about defense. You also saw common mistakes: calling too early, ignoring riichi, discarding carelessly, and not checking for yaku. These lessons will save you thousands of points.

walkthrough.title: Before Your First Real Game
walkthrough.text:
Before you jump into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics on tile efficiency and when to push versus fold.

Second — reference the Appendix for a complete list of yaku, tile types, and terminology.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

bullets: Scripted hand with 4 players: East, South, West, North
Focus on East's perspective as dealer
Learn the flow: draw, think, discard

bullets: Dealer starts with 14 tiles — sort by suit
Look for partial melds and pairs
Pair of Red Dragons = great start toward yakuhai

Lone non-relevant wind tiles are safe early discards
Place discards face up in neat rows

bullets: West calls chi on the first available tile

Keep the triplet hidden for now

Keep connectors (adjacent tiles), discard isolated tiles

summary.title: Reading the Discards

3 copies of a tile discarded → 4th is nearly impossible to get
No tiles of a suit discarded → opponents may be hoarding it

summary.title: South Declares Riichi
bullets: 1,000-point stick placed, next discard turned sideways
South is 1 tile from winning with a closed hand

summary.title: West's Mistake: Ignoring the Riichi
bullets: West discards a dangerous tile without checking safety
Riichi player has a live winning hand — careless discards risk ron
Should have checked whether the tile was safe first

bullets: Safest: tiles the riichi player already discarded (furiten rule)
Also safe: tiles discarded right after riichi by non-called players
Avoid middle tiles (4-5-6) in suits they haven't discarded

summary.title: East Decides: Push or Fold?
bullets: Close to winning + strong hand = worth pushing
Far from winning + weak hand = fold and discard safe tiles
Red Dragon triplet gives guaranteed yaku, encouraging a push

summary.title: Lesson: Push vs Fold
bullets: How close are you to winning? (1 tile = push, 3+ = fold)
How valuable is your hand? (3+ han = worth the risk)
How dangerous are your discards? (safe tiles available = push safely)
When in doubt, beginners should fold

summary.title: East Reaches Tenpai
bullets: Drawing the right tile completes the 3rd sequence
Now tenpai: 1 tile away from winning

summary.title: East Declares Riichi
bullets: Place 1,000-point stick, discard sideways
Hand is locked — cannot change tiles, only draw and discard
Riichi (1 han) + Yakuhai (1 han) = 2+ han guaranteed

summary.title: North's Mistake: Discarding into Riichi
bullets: North discards without checking safety → East calls ron
With 2 players in riichi, North should have been extra cautious
Only safe tiles should be discarded when riichi is active

bullets: Every discard should be a conscious decision
Scan: is this tile in the riichi player's discard pool?
Careless discards are the #1 way beginners lose points

summary.title: Scoring the Win
bullets: Riichi (1 han) + Yakuhai (1 han) + dora (1 han) = 3 han
Ron win: only the discarder (North) pays
Dealer wins receive higher payment

summary.title: After the Hand
bullets: Dealer won → stays dealer (renchan), bonus counter added
All tiles washed, walls rebuilt, new hand begins
Game continues until all rounds complete

summary.title: Common Beginner Trap: No Yaku Hand
bullets: Declaring a win with no yaku = chombo (false win penalty)
Always confirm at least 1 yaku before declaring
Opening your hand with chi/pon removes many easy yaku

summary.title: Common Beginner Trap: Forgetting Furiten
bullets: Winning tile in your own discard pile = furiten (no ron allowed)
Always scan your own discards before calling ron
Furiten only blocks ron — you can still win by tsumo

summary.title: Common Beginner Trap: Opening for No Reason
bullets: Don't call chi/pon just because a tile fits — ask if it's worth it
Does this bring me significantly closer to winning?
Do I still have a valid yaku after opening?

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You've seen draw/discard flow, riichi, ron, tsumo, and defense
Key mistakes: calling too early, ignoring riichi, careless discards, no yaku
These lessons will save you thousands of points

summary.title: Before Your First Real Game
bullets: Check out General Tips → tile efficiency and push vs fold strategy
Reference the Appendix → complete yaku list and terminology
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the most common beginner mistakes. First, opening your hand too early by calling chi or pon when you don't have a guaranteed yaku — this locks out riichi, pinfu, and many other scoring patterns. Second, ignoring defense when an opponent declares riichi, leading to expensive ron penalties. Third, holding onto honor tiles too long hoping to get a triplet when the chances are slim. Fourth, not checking your own discards before calling ron, resulting in a furiten violation. Fifth, declaring a win without confirming you have at least one yaku. Keep these in mind and you'll avoid the biggest point losses.

walkthrough.title: Tile Efficiency Basics
walkthrough.text: Tile efficiency is the most important skill in Riichi Mahjong. The idea is to maximize the number of different tiles that improve your hand with each discard. A pair like five-five of Circles has two tiles that turn it into a triplet. But a connector like four-five of Circles has eight tiles that complete a sequence — the three and the six, with four copies each. Middle tiles like four, five, and six have the highest connectivity because they can extend in both directions. Prioritize keeping connected tiles and discard isolated ones first.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Yaku Early
walkthrough.text: From your very first hand, think about which yaku you are heading toward. If your starting tiles are mostly in the two through eight range, aim for tanyao. If you have a pair of dragons, aim for yakuhai. If you have lots of sequences shaping up in a closed hand, think about pinfu. And if nothing else fits, remember that riichi is always available as long as you keep your hand closed. Having a yaku plan by your third or fourth discard keeps your hand focused instead of wandering aimlessly.

walkthrough.title: When to Declare Riichi
walkthrough.text: Riichi is powerful but not always the right play. Declare riichi when your hand is worth pushing — at least one or two additional han, or when you have a good multi-sided wait that is likely to complete before opponents. Do not declare riichi when you are in last place and need a big hand; riichi locks your hand so you cannot improve it further. Also avoid riichi late in the hand when the wall is almost empty, because you are paying one thousand points for a bet you may never collect. As a beginner rule of thumb, if you are tenpai with a closed hand and no other yaku, declare riichi. It is free value.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Play Fundamentals
walkthrough.text: Defense keeps you in the game. When an opponent declares riichi, your first priority is identifying safe tiles. Tiles the riichi player has already discarded are one hundred percent safe — they cannot ron on them due to furiten. Honor tiles that have already appeared three times are safe since there is only one copy left and the riichi player might not be waiting on it. Suji is a useful pattern: if someone discarded a four, the one and seven in the same suit are safer because a four-five-six wait would be furiten. When you need to fold completely, discard in order from safest to least safe and accept that some hands you will simply not win.

walkthrough.title: The Push vs Fold Decision
walkthrough.text: Every hand requires you to decide whether to push toward your own win or fold defensively. Consider three factors. First, how close are you to tenpai? If you need one more tile, pushing is often correct. If you need three or more tiles, fold. Second, what is your hand worth? A hand with three or more han justifies more risk than a one-han hand. Third, how many opponents are pushing? Against one riichi player, moderate risk is fine. Against two, fold unless your hand is very strong. As a beginner, err on the side of folding — the points you save by not dealing into a ron are just as valuable as the points you win.

walkthrough.title: Dealer Strategy
walkthrough.text: Being the dealer is a significant advantage in Riichi Mahjong. As the dealer, you receive one and a half times the normal payment when you win, and you keep dealing as long as you keep winning or stay tenpai at a draw. This means you should play more aggressively as the dealer — push harder for wins, and declare riichi more freely since the payoff is larger. Even a one-han dealer tsumo is respectable because the payment multiplier stacks. If you are tenpai as the dealer at a draw, you keep your seat and add a bonus counter, so staying tenpai is especially valuable.

walkthrough.title: Point Situation Awareness
walkthrough.text: In the later hands of the South round, the point standings become critical. If you are in first place with a comfortable lead, play defensively — avoid dealing into opponents and take small safe wins to maintain your lead. If you are in last place, you need to take risks and go for big hands. Look for opportunities to build three or four han hands, or aim for honitsu or chinitsu for large point swings. The player in second place should watch the leader closely and look for chances to overtake without falling to third. Always know the point standings before deciding your strategy for each hand.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't open your hand without a guaranteed yaku
Don't ignore defense when an opponent declares riichi
Don't hold isolated honors too long hoping for triplets
Don't call ron without checking furiten (your own discards)
Don't declare a win without confirming you have at least 1 yaku

summary.title: Tile Efficiency Basics

Connectors (4-5) accept 8 tiles vs pairs (5-5) accept 2
Middle tiles (4-5-6) have highest connectivity
Discard isolated tiles first, keep connected groups

summary.title: Plan Your Yaku Early

Dragon pair → aim for yakuhai
Sequences forming in closed hand → aim for pinfu
Nothing else? Keep hand closed → riichi is always available

summary.title: When to Declare Riichi
bullets: Good multi-sided wait + reasonable han → declare riichi
Avoid riichi late in hand (wall almost empty)
Avoid riichi when you need to improve hand further
Beginner rule: tenpai + closed + no other yaku → always riichi

summary.title: Defensive Play Fundamentals
bullets: Riichi player's own discards = 100% safe (furiten)
Suji: if 4 discarded, 1 and 7 same suit are safer
3 copies of an honor visible → 4th is safer to discard
When folding, discard safest tiles first and accept no win

summary.title: The Push vs Fold Decision
bullets: 1 tile from tenpai → push; 3+ tiles away → fold
Hand worth 3+ han → more risk justified
2+ opponents pushing → fold unless your hand is very strong
Beginners: when in doubt, fold — saved points = earned points

summary.title: Dealer Strategy
bullets: Dealer receives 1.5× payment on wins
Play more aggressively as dealer — bigger payoff
Staying tenpai at a draw keeps dealer seat + bonus counter
Even small dealer wins are valuable due to multiplier

summary.title: Point Situation Awareness
bullets: Leading: play safe, take small wins, protect your lead
Trailing: take risks, go for big hands (honitsu, chinitsu)
Know the standings before deciding strategy each hand
South round placement battles decide the game

walkthrough.title: Advanced Tile Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Beyond basic connectivity, advanced tile efficiency considers the shape of your hand as a whole. Count your shanten — the number of tiles you need to reach tenpai. A hand that is two-shanten needs two useful draws to become tenpai. Every discard should reduce your shanten or at worst maintain it. When choosing between two tiles to discard at the same shanten count, compare how many different tiles would improve your hand after each option. The discard that leaves the most improving tiles is the efficient choice.

walkthrough.title: Wait Selection and Quality
walkthrough.text: Not all tenpai waits are equal. A two-sided wait like three-four waiting on two or five has eight live tiles. A single tile wait, like waiting on one specific tile, has at most four live tiles, and possibly fewer if some have been discarded. Edge waits on one or nine are weaker since only four tiles satisfy them. When you have a choice about which meld to complete last, aim for the wait that maximizes your winning tiles. A good wait can be the difference between winning and drawing the hand.

walkthrough.title: Suji and Kabe Defense
walkthrough.text: Suji defense uses mathematical patterns to identify safer discards. If an opponent in riichi discarded a one, the four in that suit is safer because a one-two-three sequence wait would make the one discard furiten. The suji pairs are one-four, two-five, three-six, four-seven, five-eight, and six-nine. Kabe means wall — if all four copies of a tile are visible, no one can have it, and adjacent waits involving that tile are impossible. Combining suji and kabe reading gives you a much more nuanced sense of what is safe to discard.

walkthrough.title: Hand Value Optimization
walkthrough.text: Experienced players think about hand value from the start. Instead of just completing any four melds and a pair, consider which composition yields the most han. Combining pinfu plus tanyao in a closed hand gives you two han before riichi. Adding riichi and a dora puts you at four han — mangan territory for a huge payout. When you have flexibility in which melds to pursue, choose the path that maximizes your han total. Sometimes it is worth taking a slightly less efficient path if it adds a yaku.

walkthrough.title: Honitsu and Chinitsu Strategy
walkthrough.text: Honitsu is one suit plus honor tiles, worth three han closed. Chinitsu is one suit only, worth six han closed. These are the big-value hands that can swing a game. If your starting hand has seven or more tiles in one suit, consider going for honitsu. Discard tiles from the other two suits while collecting the target suit and useful honors. The downside is that opponents can read your discards and figure out your suit, making it harder to win by ron. Chinitsu hands are rare but devastating — a six-han closed hand can reach mangan or haneman easily.

walkthrough.title: Kan Strategy
walkthrough.text: Declaring kan is a double-edged sword. The upside is that you get a replacement draw from the dead wall and a new dora indicator is flipped, potentially adding dora to your hand or even giving you the winning tile. The downside is that a new dora indicator also helps your opponents. Use closed kan freely when you are ahead or pushing — it costs you nothing and might add dora. Be cautious with added kan, because opponents can declare a special ron called chankan on the tile you add. Avoid kan when you are defending, because it reveals information and adds dora that could help the riichi player.

walkthrough.title: Ippatsu, Haitei, and Situational Yaku
walkthrough.text: Several yaku depend on timing rather than hand composition. Ippatsu adds one han if you win within one full turn cycle after declaring riichi, before any calls interrupt. Haitei gives one han for winning on the very last drawable tile from the wall. Houtei gives one han for winning off the very last discard. Knowing these exist lets you factor in timing. For example, if you are riichi and an opponent calls pon before your next draw, ippatsu is broken. These situational yaku can bump a hand from two han to three, crossing a scoring threshold.

walkthrough.title: Reading Opponents' Hands
walkthrough.text: Advanced players reconstruct opponents' likely hands from discards and calls. If someone opens with a pon of Red Dragons, they have yakuhai secured and are probably building a fast hand. If someone's discards show no Bamboo tiles at all, they might be going for honitsu or chinitsu in Bamboo. Early honor discards suggest a hand focused on simples — possibly tanyao or pinfu. Late honor discards after a riichi suggest the player was holding those honors as safe tiles. Every discard tells a story, and reading that story gives you a defensive edge.

walkthrough.title: Placement Strategy in South Round
walkthrough.text: In the South round, shift your thinking from hand-by-hand to game-level strategy. If you are in first place, play for small safe wins or draws where you are tenpai. If you are in last place, go for broke — target mangan or higher hands even if they take longer to build. Second place should play aggressively against first when an opportunity arises but defensively against third and fourth. In the final hand as dealer, staying tenpai forces a renchan, giving you another chance. Point calculations become more important than hand aesthetics in the endgame.

walkthrough.title: Betaori: Complete Fold Strategy
walkthrough.text: Betaori is the art of the complete fold — abandoning your own hand to avoid dealing into an opponent's win. When you decide to fold, prioritize discards in this order: tiles the riichi player already discarded, then suji-safe tiles relative to their discards, then tiles with kabe protection, then isolated honors, and finally dangerous middle tiles only as a last resort. A good fold wins you zero points but also loses you zero. In Riichi Mahjong, not losing is just as important as winning. The best players fold cleanly when the situation calls for it.

Every discard should reduce or maintain shanten
Compare tile acceptance counts between discard options
Higher acceptance = more efficient hand

summary.title: Wait Selection and Quality
bullets: Two-sided wait (e.g., 3-4 waiting on 2/5) = 8 tiles
Single tile wait = max 4 tiles, often fewer
Edge waits (1 or 9) are weakest
Shape your hand to end with the best possible wait

summary.title: Suji and Kabe Defense

Kabe: all 4 copies visible → adjacent waits impossible
Combine suji + kabe for nuanced safety reads

summary.title: Hand Value Optimization
bullets: Plan yaku combinations from the start
Pinfu + tanyao + riichi + dora = mangan (4 han)
Sometimes a less efficient path is worth more han
Crossing scoring thresholds (mangan, haneman) is huge

summary.title: Honitsu and Chinitsu Strategy

Chinitsu (1 suit only): 6 han closed / 5 open
7+ tiles in one suit → consider honitsu

New dora indicator also helps opponents
Added kan is risky — opponents can declare chankan

summary.title: Betaori: Complete Fold Strategy
bullets: Priority: riichi player's discards → suji-safe → kabe → honors → last resort
A clean fold = 0 points lost (better than dealing into ron)
Not losing is as important as winning
Best players fold cleanly when the situation demands it

entries.definition: One of the three suited tile types. Numbered one through nine, with four copies of each. Identified by Chinese numerals with a character symbol on the bottom of the tile.
One of the three suited tile types. Numbered one through nine, with four copies of each. Identified by round dot patterns arranged on the tile face, similar to dominoes.
One of the three suited tile types. Numbered one through nine, with four copies of each. Identified by stick or rod patterns. The one of Bamboo typically shows a bird rather than a single stick.
Four types of honor tiles: East, South, West, and North. Each has four copies for sixteen wind tiles total. Used for yakuhai scoring when matching the round wind or your seat wind.

Three types of honor tiles: White (blank or bordered), Green (prosperity character), and Red (center character). Each has four copies for twelve dragon tiles total. A triplet of any dragon is always a yaku.
The ones and nines of each suit. Terminals can only form sequences in one direction (1-2-3 or 7-8-9), making them less flexible. Several yaku involve or exclude terminals.
Tiles numbered two through eight in any suit. These are the most flexible tiles for forming sequences. The yaku tanyao requires all tiles to be simples.
The collective name for all wind tiles and dragon tiles. There are twenty-eight honor tiles total. Honors cannot form sequences — only triplets or pairs.

A one-han yaku declared when your closed hand is tenpai. You place a one thousand point stick as a bet, and your hand is locked — you must discard every drawn tile unless it completes your win. Enables ura dora and ippatsu.
A one-han yaku for winning by self-draw with a fully closed hand. This is one of the simplest yaku to achieve — just keep your hand closed and draw your winning tile from the wall.
A one-han yaku requiring all tiles in the hand to be simples (numbered two through eight). No terminals or honors allowed. Works in both open and closed hands in most rule sets.
A one-han closed-hand-only yaku. All four melds must be sequences. The pair cannot be dragons, round wind, or seat wind. The final wait must be a two-sided open wait.
A one-han closed-hand-only yaku for having two identical sequences in the same suit, such as two copies of 3-4-5 of Bamboo. The hand must be fully closed.
A one-han yaku for each triplet of value tiles: any dragon tile, the current round wind, or your seat wind. Multiple yakuhai can stack. Works open or closed.
A one-han yaku for winning by drawing the very last tile from the live wall. A rare situational yaku that depends entirely on timing.
A one-han yaku for winning by ron on the very last discard of the hand. Like haitei, this is a timing-dependent situational yaku.
A one-han bonus yaku that applies only after declaring riichi. You must win within one complete turn cycle of your riichi declaration, and no calls (chi, pon, kan) can have occurred in between.
A two-han yaku for declaring riichi on your very first discard of the hand, before any calls have been made. Extremely rare but worth double the normal riichi.
A two-han yaku (one han if open) requiring every meld and the pair to contain at least one terminal or honor tile. Sequences must include a one or nine.
A two-han yaku (one han if open) for having the same sequence in all three suits, such as 4-5-6 in Characters, Circles, and Bamboo simultaneously.
A two-han yaku (one han if open) for having sequences 1-2-3, 4-5-6, and 7-8-9 all in the same suit. This covers all nine numbers in one suit across three melds.
A two-han yaku for having all four melds be triplets (no sequences). Works open or closed, but is almost always played open since forming four triplets from draws alone is very difficult.
A two-han yaku for having three concealed triplets in your hand. The fourth meld can be anything. If you win by ron using one of the triplet tiles, that triplet might not count as concealed.
A two-han yaku for having triplets of two different dragon types plus a pair of the third dragon. This also scores two additional han from the two dragon yakuhai triplets.

A two-han closed-hand-only yaku. The hand consists of seven different pairs instead of the standard four melds plus one pair. All seven pairs must be distinct — no four of a kind. Always scores 25 fu.
A three-han yaku (two han if open) for a hand containing only one suit plus honor tiles. One of the most reliable high-value yaku. Opponents can often read your suit from your discards.
A three-han yaku (two han if open) similar to chanta, but every meld and the pair must contain a terminal. No honor tiles allowed — only ones and nines as the terminal requirement.
A three-han closed-hand-only yaku for having two sets of identical sequences. Effectively iipeiko doubled. Example: two copies each of 2-3-4 Circles and 6-7-8 Bamboo.
A six-han yaku (five han if open) for a hand composed entirely of one suit with no honor tiles. The highest-value non-yakuman yaku. Difficult to build but devastating when completed.
A yakuman hand containing one of each terminal and honor tile (thirteen unique tiles) plus one duplicate. Worth the maximum base score. One of the three standard yakuman patterns.
A yakuman hand with four concealed triplets plus a pair, all formed from self-drawn tiles. If you win by ron, the final triplet may not count as concealed, so tsumo is usually required.
A yakuman hand containing triplets of all three dragon tiles — White, Green, and Red. The remaining meld and pair can be anything. Extremely powerful but telegraphed by open dragon calls.
A meld of three consecutive tiles in the same suit, such as 4-5-6 of Circles. Only suited tiles can form sequences. Sequences formed by calling chi are open; sequences drawn naturally are closed.
A meld of three identical tiles, such as three copies of the 7 of Bamboo. Can be formed by calling pon (open) or by drawing all three naturally (closed). Any tile type can form a triplet.

A set of four identical tiles declared as a meld. Counts as a triplet for hand structure but earns extra fu. Three types: closed kan, open kan, and added kan. Triggers a dead wall draw and new dora indicator.
Two identical tiles forming the head of the winning hand. Every standard winning hand requires exactly one pair in addition to four melds. Also called the head or eyes of the hand.
The state of being one tile away from completing a winning hand. A player in tenpai can declare riichi if their hand is closed, and receives points from non-tenpai players during a draw.
The number of tiles needed to reach tenpai. A hand that is two-shanten needs two useful draws. Zero-shanten means you are tenpai. Reducing shanten efficiently is the core skill of Mahjong.

A state where you cannot win by ron because a tile that would complete your hand is in your own discard pile. Furiten players can only win by tsumo. Temporary furiten occurs when you pass on a ron opportunity.
Scoring multipliers earned from yaku and dora. Each han roughly doubles the hand's base point value. More han means dramatically higher payouts, especially at scoring thresholds like mangan.
Base points calculated from hand composition including wait type, meld types (open vs closed triplets), and winning method. Combined with han in the scoring formula. Rounded up to the nearest ten.

A scoring threshold reached at three han with seventy or more fu, or four han with thirty or more fu, or five han with any fu. Pays a fixed amount: 4,000 from each non-dealer for a dealer tsumo, or 8,000/12,000 for ron.
A scoring threshold for hands worth six or seven han. Pays one and a half times mangan. A haneman hand is a major win that can swing the game significantly.
A scoring threshold for hands worth eight to ten han. Pays double mangan. Extremely rare in casual play but achievable with multiple yaku and dora stacking.

The highest scoring threshold, reached at thirteen or more han or by completing a specific yakuman pattern. Pays four times mangan. Yakuman hands are the legendary hands of Mahjong — powerful and exceedingly rare.
Bonus tiles indicated by the dora indicator on the dead wall. The tile next in sequence after the indicator is the dora. Each dora in a winning hand adds one han. Not a yaku by itself.

Additional dora tiles revealed only when winning with riichi. The tiles underneath each dora indicator on the dead wall are flipped to determine ura dora. Can dramatically increase a riichi win's value.
Winning by drawing your winning tile from the wall. All three opponents pay. Non-dealers pay the base amount; the dealer pays double. A self-drawn win with a closed hand is itself a one-han yaku.
Winning by claiming another player's discarded tile. Only the discarder pays the full value of the hand. Ron cannot be declared if you are in furiten. Has the highest priority among all tile claims.
A penalty for an illegal action, most commonly declaring a false win (no yaku or furiten ron). The offending player typically pays a mangan-equivalent penalty to all other players.
A dealer repeat. Occurs when the dealer wins the hand or is tenpai at an exhaustive draw. A bonus counter (honba) is added, increasing the stakes of the next hand by three hundred points per counter.
Counters that accumulate when the dealer repeats. Each honba adds three hundred points to the winner's payment. Counters reset when a non-dealer wins a hand.
The first round of the game. The round wind is East, and each player serves as dealer once (minimum four hands). Some games play only the East round for a shorter experience.
The second round of the game. The round wind changes to South, and each player again serves as dealer once. The game normally ends after the South round.
Fourteen tiles set aside during setup that are never drawn during normal play. Contains the dora indicators and replacement tiles for kan draws. The dead wall is always maintained at fourteen tiles.
Point sticks used to track each player's score throughout the game. Come in denominations of ten thousand, five thousand, one thousand, and one hundred. Exchanged between players after each hand.
A call to claim a discarded tile from the player to your left to form a sequence. The completed sequence is placed face up, opening your hand. Only the player whose turn would be next can call chi.
A call to claim any player's discarded tile when you hold two matching tiles, forming a triplet. The triplet is placed face up, opening your hand. Has higher priority than chi.

A defensive technique using mathematical tile relationships. The suji pairs are 1-4, 2-5, 3-6, 4-7, 5-8, 6-9. If a riichi player discarded one tile of a suji pair, the other is statistically safer to discard.
A defensive concept. When all four copies of a tile are visible (discarded or in open melds), that tile forms a wall — no one can be waiting on it, and certain adjacent waits become impossible.

The strategy of completely abandoning your own hand to play purely defensively. Discard only safe tiles to avoid dealing into an opponent's win. A critical survival skill when opponents are threatening.
